intitule: Lesson 7 Evaluation: Obligation and Advice (must, have to, should, ought to)

enonce.texte: Read each sentence carefully. Choose the correct modal auxiliary (must, have to, should, ought to) based on the context. Pay attention to whether the obligation comes from the speaker (internal) or from a rule/external source.

questions.texte: In Cameroon, all drivers ______ carry a valid driver's license and insurance papers.
You look very tired. You ______ take a break from studying.
I ______ finish this project tonight because the boss needs it tomorrow morning. It's not my choice.
I ______ remember to call my mother on her birthday. It is a matter of personal respect.
You ______ respect the elders in your community. It is a fundamental rule of our society.
People ______ smoke in government buildings; it is strictly prohibited by law.

intitule: Lesson 7: Problem Solving in Real Life Contexts

enonce.texte: Read the three real-life situations below. For each situation, answer the questions by applying the rules of must, have to, should, and ought to. Justify your choice.
images: A student feeling sick in a classroom in Cameroon with a teacher looking concerned.
A group of students in a school library in Yaoundé with one student talking loudly on a phone.
A candidate standing at a polling station in Cameroon holding a voter card during an election.

questions.texte: Situation 1: You are a student at a lycée in Douala. You feel very dizzy and have a high fever. You have an important Math test in 10 minutes.

sousQuestions.texte: Formulate a piece of advice for yourself using 'should' or 'ought to'.
sousQuestions.reponse: I should go to the school infirmary first./I ought to tell the teacher I am sick.

sousQuestions.texte: Why would you use 'must' instead of 'have to' in this context if you decide to see the nurse?
sousQuestions.reponse: I would use 'must' because it expresses a personal and urgent necessity (I must see a doctor now). It comes from my internal feeling of urgency, not an external rule.

questions.texte: Situation 2: During the end-of-year exams, the school principal announces a new regulation: "All mobile phones ______ be turned off and kept in your bags."

sousQuestions.texte: Fill in the blank with the correct modal expressing external obligation.
Explain why 'must' would be less appropriate in the principal's official announcement.
sousQuestions.reponse: 'Have to' is more appropriate for rules and regulations imposed by an authority (the school). 'Must' is often used for personal obligations or strong internal feelings, or in very formal legal texts. While possible, 'have to' is standard for external rules.

questions.texte: Situation 3: A candidate at a polling station in Bamenda wants to vote. The electoral code states: "Every citizen ______ present a valid national ID card before voting."

sousQuestions.texte: Choose the best modal for this official regulation and justify.
sousQuestions.reponse: 'Must' is very strong and appropriate for official written regulations or laws. 'Have to' is also acceptable. However, in formal legal contexts, 'must' is often preferred to indicate an absolute requirement.

sousQuestions.texte: A friend tells you: 'You don't have to vote if you are tired.' Is this correct? Explain the difference between 'don't have to' and 'mustn't'.
sousQuestions.reponse: Yes, it is grammatically correct. 'Don't have to' means it is not necessary (no obligation). 'Mustn't' means it is prohibited (negative obligation). So, voting is not compulsory, but if you choose to vote, you must follow the rules.
